coredata.dc:description: Colletotrichum gloeosporioides, the primary fungal pathogen responsible for mango anthracnose, causes significant economic losses. Hence, we developed a xanthan gum/hydroxypropyl methylcellulose (XG/HPMC) film incorporating Dictyophora echinovolvata carbon quantum dots (DE-CQDs). These green-synthesized DE-CQDs are 2 nm spherical nanoparticles with good crystallinity and thermal stability, featuring functional groups such as -OH, -COOH, -NH₄+, and -SO₃H, which enhance hydrophilicity, solubility, and charge properties. The negative charge of DE-CQDs, as evidenced by zeta potential measurements, enhances their dispersibility and inhibits aggregation, thereby contributing to the film's augmented UV-blocking and water vapor transmission characteristics. DE-CQDs inhibit C. gloeosporioides growth through a unique mechanism, inducing metacaspase-dependent apoptosis and causing ROS accumulation without DNA fragmentation. When incorporated into the XHC film, DE-CQDs improve UV-blocking by 30 % at 400 nm, enhance water vapor transmission, and increase water contact angle. The XHC film significantly reduces mango decay (decay index: 0.35 ± 0.05 to 0.21 ± 0.05) and boosts vitamin C retention (from 30 % to 61 % over 7 days), effectively extending shelf life and preserving nutritional quality. Among the XHC films, XHC6 shows the best overall performance. Thus, DE-CQDs serve as functional fillers in biodegradable, environmentally friendly packaging films, offering a novel solution for mango disease prevention and control.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Colletotrichum gloeosporioides, the primary fungal pathogen responsible for mango anthracnose, causes significant economic losses. Hence, we developed a xanthan gum/hydroxypropyl methylcellulose (XG/HPMC) film incorporating Dictyophora echinovolvata carbon quantum dots (DE-CQDs). These green-synthesized DE-CQDs are 2 nm spherical nanoparticles with good crystallinity and thermal stability, featuring functional groups such as -OH, -COOH, -NH₄+, and -SO₃H, which enhance hydrophilicity, solubility, and charge properties. The negative charge of DE-CQDs, as evidenced by zeta potential measurements, enhances their dispersibility and inhibits aggregation, thereby contributing to the film's augmented UV-blocking and water vapor transmission characteristics. DE-CQDs inhibit C. gloeosporioides growth through a unique mechanism, inducing metacaspase-dependent apoptosis and causing ROS accumulation without DNA fragmentation. When incorporated into the XHC film, DE-CQDs improve UV-blocking by 30 % at 400 nm, enhance water vapor transmission, and increase water contact angle. The XHC film significantly reduces mango decay (decay index: 0.35 ± 0.05 to 0.21 ± 0.05) and boosts vitamin C retention (from 30 % to 61 % over 7 days), effectively extending shelf life and preserving nutritional quality. Among the XHC films, XHC6 shows the best overall performance. Thus, DE-CQDs serve as functional fillers in biodegradable, environmentally friendly packaging films, offering a novel solution for mango disease prevention and control.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mango is an important economic crop, primarily grown in tropical and subtropical regions of Asia, Australia, and Africa [1]. According to the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), global mango production reaches 57 million tons, with India (43.75 %), China (6.95 %), and Indonesia (6.24 %) being the leading producers. However, mangoes are highly susceptible to anthracnose disease caused by Colletotrichum gloeosporioides (C. gloeosporioides) after harvest, resulting in losses of up to 30 %–50 % during storage [2]. In fact, C. gloeosporioides is among the top ten harmful fungal pathogens in plant pathology [3]. While chemical control methods are commonly employed, they pose residual toxicity risks to food, complicating pathogen management [4]. Moreover, excessive reliance on chemical fungicides can lead to fungal resistance [5]. Thus, there is an urgent need for effective and environmentally friendly antifungal agents to control anthracnose and preserve mango quality.

Research aimed at extending mango shelf life has focused on developing novel antifungal agents and food packaging materials. Traditional packaging materials often fail to provide effective antifungal protection, allowing fungi to continue reproducing during storage [6]. A variety of strategies have been investigated, encompassing the utilization of bioactive constituents such as essential oils and plant extracts abundant in phenolics, terpenes, and aliphatic alcohols, which demonstrate considerable antifungal potential. For example, Artemisia scoparia essential oil disrupts the mycelium of C. gloeosporioides, leading to leakage of fungal contents and tube formation [7]. Similarly, Ganoderma lucidum extract damages the fungal cell membrane and inhibits conidial germination [8]. Furthermore, Bacillus amyloliquefaciens enhances mango's latent resistance to anthracnose by activating reactive oxygen species (ROS) [9]. However, these natural antifungal agents may lead to pathogen resistance, and their antifungal activity often decreases with repeated use, highlighting the need for more sustainable solutions [5,8].

In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have gained attention as a promising antifungal agent and food packaging material. These nanocarbon particles (<10 nm) exhibit chemical stability, antibacterial properties, and good biocompatibility, making them suitable for various biotechnological applications, including sensors and bioimaging [10]. CQDs demonstrate significant potential in the domain of food packaging, wherein they contribute to prolonging the shelf life of products by imparting antifungal protection [11]. Importantly, CQDs may be synthesized from renewable resources, including microorganisms, agricultural residues, and food by-products, as well as from routine food processing activities such as cooking [12,13]. The selection of starting materials is critical for enhancing the antifungal properties of the resulting CQDs [14].

Biomass-derived materials, such as the agricultural by-product Dictyophora indusiata (commonly known as “Zhu sun”), are increasingly used to produce CQDs due to their rich organic compound content, including proteins, polysaccharides, and volatile oils [15]. The residual issue of CQDs can be resolved through biodegradation [14]. In our previous research, CQDs synthesized from Dictyophora indusiata (DE-CQDs) exhibited significant inhibitory effects against plant pathogens [16]. DE-CQDs can be derived from agricultural waste, enhancing resource utilization and offering environmentally friendly alternatives for food packaging materials [17]. This methodology not only contributes to the advancement of a circular economy by converting agricultural by-products into valuable resources but also mitigates the environmental impact associated with non-biodegradable plastic packaging. Considering the promising antifungal properties of DE-CQDs, it is hypothesized that they possess the capability to inhibit the growth of C. gloeosporioides, notwithstanding the scarcity of research on their antifungal potential against this particular pathogen. This research gap necessitates further investigation into the specific antifungal properties and mechanisms of DE-CQDs, particularly their ability to inhibit fungal growth and induce cell apoptosis.

The use of CQDs as antibacterial coatings in food preservation has been a growing area of interest [18]. Antibacterial mechanisms of CQDs include covalent and noncovalent interactions, ROS generation, photothermal therapy, and size effects [19]. However, the exact antifungal mechanism remains underexplored. Some studies suggest that CQDs interact with negative charges on the fungal cell wall or with ergosterol, promoting ROS accumulation and DNA degradation [14]. Other research indicates that CQDs' functional groups can bind to cell wall components, causing membrane perforation and compromising fungal cell integrity [20]. In this study, we observe that DE-CQDs induce apoptosis in C. gloeosporioides, a highly efficient mechanism that is less likely to lead to drug resistance [21], warranting further investigation of the underlying mechanisms.

To maximize CQDs' potential in food preservation, selecting an effective matrix for optimal loading and performance is essential. Based on previous work, xanthan gum/hydroxypropyl methylcellulose (XG/HPMC) has demonstrated excellent film-forming and loading capabilities [22,23]. In this study, we developed an innovative, novel antifungal coating film (XG/HPMC/DE-CQDs, XHC) to inhibit C. gloeosporioides growth and extend mango shelf life, fully aligning with food safety and environmental protection standards. We aim to elucidate the inhibitory effects and mechanism of DE-CQDs in inducing cell apoptosis in C. gloeosporioides, thereby providing insights into the preservation mechanism of XHC films. Ultimately, we hope to contribute to the development of active packaging materials for the food packaging industry, with DE-CQDs serving as an effective, biocompatible, and sustainable antifungal agent.

The D. echinovolvata pilei was collected from Shunchang, Fujian, cleaned, dried, crushed, and sieved with a 40 mesh sieve. XG (USP; CAS: 11138–66-2; Mw is 3374 Da; the viscosity of 1 % XG in 1 % KCl at 24.9 °C under 60 rpm is 1500 cp) was purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation. Hydroxypropyl methylcellulose (HPMC) (CAS: 9004-65-3; Mw is 4237 Da; the viscosity of 1 % HPMC in 1 % KCl at 25 °C under 60 rpm is 9 cp; methoxyl: 19.6 %; and hydroxypropoxyl: 30.3 %) was purchased from Shenzhen Zhenxinjia Trade Co., LTD. Sorbitol and glycerin were purchased from Sangon Biotech and Xilong Scientific, respectively; all are food grade. The mango used in this research was harvested from Haikou, Hainan Province, and purchased from the Fuzhou wholesale fruit market. The genomic DNA kit was purchased from Beijing Bioteke Co., Ltd. The green fluorescent dye dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), the TdT-mediated dUTP Nick-End Labeling (TUNEL), and the caspase inhibitor Z-VAD-FMK were purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology.

15 g powder from pilei from D. echinovolvata was evenly dispersed with 150 mL of ultrapure water in a 250 mL polyvinyl fluoride reaction kettle, and the hydrothermal reaction was performed at 180 °C for 8 h in an oven (Shanghai Yiheng Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd.). The filter was then filtered with a 0.22 μm nylon filter head after natural cooling. Next, it was dialyzed with a dialysis bag (1000 Da) for 24 h, and ultrapure water from Youpu series ultra-pure water device (UPH-IV-201, Chengdu Ultra Pure Technology Co., Ltd., China) was replaced every 8 h. DE-CQDs were obtained after the sample in the dialysis bag was frozen and dried (Christ ALPHA 1–2 LD plus, Germany).

DE-CQDs were characterized using multiple techniques [12,24,25]. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired using a JEOL 2100F microscope operating at 200 kV. Crystal structures were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a Rigaku Smart Lab SE, with Cu-Kα radiation, a scanning range of 5°-90°, and a scan rate of 2°/min. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was conducted using a Thermo Fisher Nicolet iS20. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha spectrometer with a 400 μm spot size, 12 kV working voltage, and 6 mA filament current. Full-spectrum scans were performed at 100 eV (step size 1 eV), and fine scans at 50 eV (step size 0.1 eV). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed using a HITACHI STA200, over a temperature range of 30–800 °C, at a heating rate of 10 °C/min and a data acquisition interval of 0.4 s. Zeta potential was measured using a Malvern ZS-90 nanoparticle size analyzer. UV absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy were performed using a BioTek and a ZF-20D system (Edinburgh Instruments).

HPMC ) of DE-CQDs were added to the XG/HPMC solution and were stirred to homogenize. 0.2 g of sorbitol and glycerin (1:1, m
sorbitol
:m
glycerin ) were then sequentially added to the solutions XG/HPMC/DE-CQDs (XHC), respectively. The samples were ultrasonically degassed for 30 min and allowed to stand for 12 h. A homogenous XHC solution devoid of bubbles was achieved. Subsequently, 20 mL of XHC solutions were dispensed onto a glass substrate and subjected to drying for 48 h at 50 °C in an oven. The films were labeled based on the DE-CQDs concentration as XHC0, XHC2 (2 % HPMC), XHC4 (4 % HPMC), XHC6 (6 % HPMC), XHC8 (8 % HPMC), and XHC10 (10 % HPMC), respectively. The resulting dried XHC film was utilized for further structural characterization. Furthermore, the uniform XHC mixture was diluted 20-fold for application in mango preservation.

The FT-IR of the XHC film was tested using an FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker Optics Co., GER) according to our previous work with a minor modification [22] using an ATR pattern with 1 cm−1 resolution. XRD (Ultima IV, Rigaku Co., Japan) of the XHC film was tested with a minor modification [22]. The 2θ was set from 10 to 60° at a scanning speed of 0.05°/min. The SEM (JSM-6380LV, JEOL, Japan) of the XHC film was tested according to our previous work [22].

Light transmittance (LT) was determined according to our previous work using a × Mark device (BioTek, Vermont, USA) [26]. Surface color was studied according to our previous work [27]. The color of the XHC film was tested by taking the average values of the five measurements for each film formulation with a colorimeter using the High-Quality Colorimeter NH310 (Shenzhen ThreeNH Technology Co., Ltd., China). The total color difference (ΔE) was determined using a standard plate (L*: 84.50; a*: 0.44; b*: −4.68).

Tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EB) of the XHC film (80 mm × 15 mm) were determined by a universal texture meter (CMT6103, MTS System Corporation, USA) according to GB/T 13022–1991, with the probe moving speed of 1 mm/s and the initial clamp distance of 50 mm [22,27]. The thickness of the XHC film was measured using a digital micrometer (Everte Group Co., Ltd., Henan, China) according to GB/T 1040.3–2006 [26]. Subsequently, the thickness measurement of the film was input into the apparatus.

The water vapor transmission rate (WVTR) of the XHC film was determined using a water contact analyzer (JY-82A video contact angle meter, Chengde Dingsheng Testing Machine Testing Equipment Co., Ltd., China) according to Liu, Song, Chen, Deng, Chao, Yang, Wu, Bai, Zhang and Hu [28] with a minor modification, the size of the XHC film is 3 cm × 3 cm, the maintainable condition is 30 °C and 80 % relative humidity. The water solubility (WS) and moisture content (MC) of the XHC film were determined by using the weight-change method. The 50 mm × 50 mm XHC film was weighed as m 1 (g). The XHC film was weighed as m 2 (g) after drying in a 100 °C blast drying oven (Shanghai Yiheng Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd.) to constant weight. Next, it was soaked in distilled water for 24 h at 25 °C. Finally, the XHC film was removed and dried in a 100 °C-blast drying oven (Shanghai Yiheng Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd., China) to constant mass, and its mass was m 3 (g). WS and MC were calculated using the following formula:

It is reported that the water contact angle (WCA) can be used to identify the surface wettability of the film [28]. The WCA of the XHC film was measured using an artificial climate incubator (HP450GS-LED, Wuhan Ruihua Instrument and Equipment Co., Ltd., China) according to our previous work [27]. Surface tension (ST) is one of the key physicochemical properties of mixed polymer solutions and can be used to determine the optimal composition [29]. ST was determined using a surface tensiometer (OCA25, DataPhysics Instruments GmbH, Germany).

To validate the biocompatibility of the XHC film, an in vitro cytotoxicity analysis was performed by employing the MTT assay on L929 mouse fibroblast cells over a 24-h period at 50 μg/mL, 100 μg/mL, 500 μg/mL, and 1000 μg/mL, following the methodology outlined by Ezati et al. [11]. Furthermore, optical micrographs were utilized to assess and contrast the morphology and viability of live cells between the control group and those subjected to the XHC films.

Mango was coated with an XHC solution according to the method of coating on banana with a minor modification [22]. Mangos were soaked in 20-fold diluted XHC for 10 min. After removing the residual solution on the surface, it was stored at 25 °C, and a distilled water coating treatment was applied as a control. The weight loss ratio (WLR), the decay rate (DR), and the decay index (DI) of mango were researched from 0 to 7 days. The DI calculated the decay area (0: 0 %; 1: 0–25 %; 2: 25–50 %; 3: >50 % decay) by infection according to the method of Huang et al. [30]. The DI was calculated as follows:
(3)
DI =
decay score × corresponding number of mangoes within each class
total number of mango × scale numbers
× 100
The titratable acidity (TA), total soluble solid (TSS) and vitamin C (Vc) of mango was measured by titration, hand-held refractometer, and assay kit, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Isolation, characterization, and molecular identification of the mango pathogen
ce:section.ce:para: Isolation and characterization of the mango pathogen were carried out according to Xiao et al. [31]. Mango disease tissue was isolated with potato dextrose agar (PDA) medium. The strains were purified by a single spore technique and then subcultured in new PDA dishes. The shape of the representative strain of conidia (FJAT-32130) was investigated using a phase contrast microscope at 100× magnification (BX43F, TOKYO, Japan). FJAT-32130 was used for molecular identification. The total DNA of FJAT-32130 was extracted using a Genomic DNA Kit (Tiangen, Beijing, China). The ITS amplification procedure: pre-denaturation for 5 min at 94 °C, denaturation for 1 min at 94 °C, annealing for 1 min at 55 °C, elongation at 72 °C for 1 min, and final elongation for 10 min at 72 °C (Bio-Rad, USA). The amplified products were observed by ultraviolet (UV) instruments after electrophoresis. PCR products were sent to a commercial sequencing service provider (Biosune, Shanghai, China) for sequencing, then the sequences were aligned with the GenBank database (http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/Blast/). To determine the strain species, the FJAT-32130 phylogenetic tree was constructed using neighbor-joining methods in MEGA version 6.0 software, the specific parameters were referred to the maximum composite likelihood model to calculate the sequence distance, and the confidence interval was analyzed using 1000 replicates of bootstrap.

The fungal inhibition effect of DE-CQDs was determined by the zone-of-inhibition assay method. The FJAT-32130 was punched into an 8 mm disc by punching. 5 mL 80 mg/mL DE-CQD was evenly added to the 5 mL PDA medium. The FJAT-32130 disc was placed in the middle of the PDA medium containing DE-CQDs and cultured at 28 °C for 7 days. The positive control and negative control were obtained using 5 mL of 0.5 mg/mL of hygromycin and 5 mL of sterile water, replacing DE-CQDs, respectively. The diameter of the inhibition zone was determined by the cross method.

The apoptosis-related assay was studied according to Deng et al. [32]. The green fluorescent dye DCFH-DA and TUNEL were used to label the FJAT-32130 hyphae, and the ROS level and the DNA breakage level were observed in the FJAT-32130 hyphae under DE-CQDs treatment, respectively. Furthermore, the caspase inhibitor Z-VAD-FMK was used to analyze whether the metacaspase of FJAT-32130 was involved in the induction of cell apoptosis.

Statistical analysis (p < 0.05) was studied using SPSS Statistics 17.0 software with Duncan's test. The value in the figures and tables is shown as the mean values ± relative deviation, and the lowercase letters refer to significantly different values.

The structural characterization of DE-CQDs is presented in Fig. 1 . These are spherical nanoparticles with a size of approximately 2 nm (Fig. 1a). TEM and XRD analysis show that DE-CQDs exhibit a distinct lattice structure with a spacing of 0.22 nm (inset of Fig. 1a) and an absorption peak at 2θ = 21.36° (Fig. 1b), corresponding to the (100) and (002) planes of graphite [33], reflecting good crystallinity. FT-IR analysis reveals that the surface of DE-CQDs contains functional groups including -OH, -COOH, -NH₄+, and -SO₃H (Fig. 1c). The main characteristic peaks are 3410 cm−1 (-OH/NH), 2926 cm−1 (CH), 1656 cm−1 (C=O), 1408 cm−1 (CN), 1078 cm−1 (CO), 1205 cm−1 (S=O), 1044 cm−1 (SO), and 620 cm−1 (SO deformation) [12,33]. XPS analysis revealed that the surface elemental composition of DE-CQDs is primarily composed of C (65.02 %), N (10.4 %), O (24.27 %), and S (0.31 %) (Fig. 1d). The structure contains functional groups such as C-C/C-H, C-O/C-N, CO, CN, NH₄+, C-O/S-O, CS, and SO₃H (Fig. 1e-h), suggesting that carbon exists not only as graphite but also covalently bonded to oxygen or nitrogen atoms [34]. These functional groups contribute to the hydrophilicity, water solubility, and charge properties of DE-CQDs.

The incorporation of -COOH and -SO₃H functional groups confers a negative charge on DE-CQDs, as corroborated by zeta potential assessments. The zeta potential of DE-CQDs measures at −9.98 ± 7.77 mV, reflecting a considerable negative charge [24]. These functional groups form the basis for superior hydration, solubility, and moisture absorption characteristics. The negative charge is pivotal in augmenting the solubility and dispersibility of DE-CQDs within the film matrix. Specifically, the negative charge induces electrostatic repulsion between DE-CQDs, thereby hindering aggregation and ensuring a more uniform distribution within the film. This uniformity is fundamental for the film's efficacy, as it enhances UV-blocking capabilities and water vapor transmission, which are vital for prolonging the shelf life of mangoes. Thermogravimetric analysis (Fig. 1i) indicates that the polymer chain cleavage temperature of DE-CQDs is 343.5 °C, with a residual mass of 22.8 % at 800 °C, confirming good thermal stability [24]. The UV–Vis spectrum shows an absorption peak at 310 nm (Fig. 1j), which is attributed to the n → π* transition of the CO bond in the DE-CQDs carbon cores [35]. The presence of the –C=O group helps to absorb the UV light [36]. DE-CQDs exhibit excitation/emission wavelengths of 398 nm/484 nm, displaying blue fluorescence (Fig. 1k) with clear wavelength dependence. The emission peak shifts from 447 nm to 520 nm, with the emission intensity increasing and then decreasing (Fig. 1l). In summary, DE-CQDs display excellent water solubility, thermal stability, and fluorescence properties. The surface is rich in functional groups, making DE-CQDs promising for applications in preservative films, sensors, and drug delivery systems. In addition, compared to previous structural characterization results [16], the structural characterization of DE-CQDs synthesized from different batches of D. echinovolvata pileus is consistent, suggesting that the carbon quantum dots prepared from this material demonstrate good stability and repeatability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the structural characteristics of the XHC film
ce:section.ce:para: The FT-IR spectra of the XHC film are shown in Fig. 2a. The spectra show a broad peak. The peaks at 3420 cm−1 and 1644 cm−1 of XHC0 are assigned to the OH and CO group stretching vibrations, respectively [22]. No deviation of the XHC spectra was observed, indicating that the added DE-CQDs did not destroy the XH. However, the peak intensity at 3402 cm−1 of XHC2 was higher due to DE-CQDs being rich in carboxyl and hydroxyl groups [16]. Meanwhile, the wave number was also shifted from 3420 cm−1 to 3402 cm−1, possibly due to DE-CQDs having destroyed the original ordered structure. The intensity of the peak at 3427 cm−1 and 3410 cm−1 of XHC4 and XHC6 was weakened, perhaps because the hydrogen bonding between DE-CQDs and XH, and the hydroxyl peak of XHC6 has a red shift (1644 cm−1 to 1652 cm−1), which shows that the hydrogen bonding is the strongest and the structure is the most stable [37]. Upon the addition of an excessive quantity of DE-CQDs, the OH peak intensity increases once more, attributed to the presence of numerous hydroxyl groups from DE-CQDs that remain unengaged in hydrogen bonding with XH. The findings suggest that the interactions between DE-CQDs and XH are predominantly non-covalent and involve hydrogen bonding, ensuring that the fundamental structure of the XHC film remains unaltered [38].

Fig. 2b shows the results of the XRD of the XHC films. A wide diffraction peak appeared at 2θ = 20.18° ~ 20.62°, indicating that XHC is amorphous [22]. The XHC film showed the same diffraction pattern without additional peaks, indicating that CQDs were uniformly distributed in the hydroxypropyl methylcellulose/xanthan gum matrix [37]. Nevertheless, as the dosage of DE-CQDs increased, the crystallinity, as indicated by the peak area, initially diminished (XHC2), subsequently augmented until reaching a point of excess, and ultimately diminished once more (XHC10). An increased dosage of DE-CQDs resulted in the formation of a greater number of hydrogen bonds between DE-CQDs and the XH film, which rearranged the internal structure of XHC0 and increased the crystallinity (Fig. 2a). However, when the concentration of DE-CQDs surpassed 8 % HPMC (m/m), a reduction in crystallinity was observed. This is attributable to the surplus DE-CQDs disrupting the inherent molecular order of the XHC film, thereby leading to an increase in the film's density [39].

As shown in Fig. 2c, DE-CQDs particles appeared on the surface of the XHC film after DE-CQDs were added [16]. It was found to be uniformly distributed in the XH, and the compatibility of the film components was good and evenly distributed. The surface of the XHC film exhibits a smooth structure, and the roughness increases slightly with an increase in the dosage of DE-CQDs. Similarly, Xie et al. were able to create the surface roughness of the potato starch/watermelon peel pectin composite film by adding nano-TiO2 [38]. The continuous internal structure of the XHC film is shown in (XHC cross section), which is an essential characterization of the XHC film [37]. The mechanism of HPMC, XG, and DE-CQDs in XHC films is shown in Scheme 1 . A continuous network was constructed through hydrogen bonds between HPMC and XG, and DE-CQDs could interact with active groups on the surface of HPMC and XG to build a dense cross-linking network. The synergistic enhancement effect after doping with various substances can improve the structure and properties of the composite material [40].

Fig. 3 shows the UV-protection performance of XHC films and DE-CQDs-loaded XHC films. As shown in Fig. 3a and Fig. 3b, it was found that the XHC films, which contain more DE-CQDs, have higher absorption and lower transmittance in 200–400 nm. The UV-blocking property was illustrated by the average transmittance of UV-A and UV-B, as described by Wang et al. [25]. Approximately 85 % of UV-A and 80 % of UV-B radiation penetrated the XHC0 film, while UV radiation was effectively blocked with increasing amounts of DE-CQDs. The UV-A and UV-B transmittance decreased to 50 % and 20 % in the XHC10 film, respectively. In other words, 50 % of UV-A and 80 % of UV-B radiation were blocked. This demonstrates that DE-CQDs are excellent UV absorbers [25]. The negative charge on DE-CQDs improves their dispersion within the film matrix, leading to a more uniform distribution of nanoparticles. This uniform distribution enhances the film's ability to block UV radiation. Higher absorption and lower transmittance indicate better UV blocking ability [38]. The film must provide effective protection at approximately 400 nm [35]. The LT (T400) values of the XHC films exhibited a significant decline with the augmenting concentration of DE-CQDs (p < 0.5) (Fig. 3c), decreasing from 87.90 ± 0.73 % (XHC0) to 61.72 ± 1.56 % (XHC10). This observation suggests that incorporating CQDs enhances the film's UV resistance by 30 %. Among the samples, XHC10 demonstrated the highest absorption intensity, signifying the superior UV blocking capability. Furthermore, the LT (T600) values of the XHC films were found to decrease with increasing DE-CQDs (Fig. 3b). However, the XHC film had a high transmittance of >80 % in the visible region at 600 nm, and the letter pattern under the XHC film can be seen clearly. These results suggested that DE-CQDs can enhance UV protection and visible light transmission due to their aggregation. The tiny DE-CQD particles fill the gaps in the film [38], while the bandgap absorption and light scattering by DE-CQD nanoparticles [36] contribute to reduced light transmission. This demonstrates that DE-CQDs are excellent UV absorbents [39]. Their strong UV barrier properties can inhibit photooxidation, prevent fruit spoilage, and extend shelf life [13,36].

It should be noted that the intensified coloration of the XHC film may lead to diminished light transmission [27]. Therefore, we investigated the impact of varying quantities on the film's coloration. The significant difference effects of DE-CQD dose on the color difference of the XHC film are shown in Fig. 3a and Table 1 . It was found that the L⁎ decreased from 84.56 ± 0.09 to 69.60 ± 0.43 with increasing DE-CQDs dosage, indicating a darker XHC film. Meanwhile, a ⁎ increased from 0.62 ± 0.02 to 6.65 ± 0.09 with an increased dose of DE-CQDs, indicating that the XHC film tended to red [39]. A significant increase in ΔE ⁎ and b ⁎ with an increase in the dose of DE-CQDs, indicating that the XHC film tended towards a yellow hue [38]. Therefore, the dosage of DE-CQDs affects ΔE ⁎ of the XHC film, which reduces the light transmission of the XHC film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of DE-CQD dosage on the mechanical properties and water resistance of the XHC film
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanical properties of the XHC film are shown in Fig. 4a. The TS of the XHC film decreased after DE-CQDs were added. This is because the DE-CQDs disrupted the original structure. Nonetheless, the TS value exhibited an incremental trend with the augmentation of DE-CQDs dosage. This phenomenon may be attributed to the interaction between CQDs and the matrix material, potentially influencing the tensile properties. [41]. This trend can be attributed to the charge properties of DE-CQDs. The negative charge on DE-CQDs enhances their interaction with the positively charged regions of HPMC and XG, leading to a more robust and uniform film structure. The addition of essential cinnamon oil to chitosan also demonstrated the same phenomenon [28]. However, there are no significant differences in the effect of different DE-CQDs additions on the EB of the XHC film. The largest TS after adding DE-CQDs of the XHC10 film was 28.73 ± 0.18 MPa. This was higher than S-PAHI-Gly30 (4.0 ± 0.8 MPa) [42], CMC-Na/QD/PU (10.80 MPa) [43], and polylactic acid/CQDs from Rosehip (17.65 MPa) [12].

The water resistance results of the XHC film are depicted in Fig. 4b-f. A pattern was noted, where initially there is an increase followed by a decrease in the WVTR, MC, and WS as the dosage of DE-CQDs increases. Among the samples, XHC6 had the highest WVTR (1.09 ± 0.13) × 10−10 g·m−1·s−1·Pa−1 (Fig. 6b), MC (12.47 ± 1.84 %) (Fig. 4c) and WS (84.41 ± 2.85 %) (Fig. 4d). The experimental results show that the DE-CQDs disperse well in the XG/HPMC, which is conducive to providing a long diffusion pathway of water vapor molecules [44]. Nevertheless, an excessive incorporation of CQDs results in a more compact and orderly film structure, accompanied by an increase in film thickness. Consequently, this impedes the permeation of water vapor through the film's pores, thereby reducing the WVTR [45]. DE-CQDs contain a large number of hydroxyl groups and are hydrophilic, which improves the water absorption of the XHC film due to the hydrophilic surface (WCA < 90°) (Fig. 4e). The binding reaction of DE-CQDs, XG, and HPMC causes a slight increase in ST (Fig. 4f). Similarly, as concluded by Elsayed et al., with the increase in ST that occurs in chitosan, it interacts with free unbound insulin or insulin bound to mesoporous silica nanoparticles [46]. CQDs and N-functionalized CQDs (synthesized using glucose as a carbon source) increased the WVTR of the base film of the cellulose nanofiber base film [37], while the hydrophilic polyphenols can increase the WVTR of the PVA composite film [47].

Referring to the analytical performance of some recently published reports, it is found that the WVTR obtained from XHC in this work is somewhat lower than chitosan-bacterial cellulose-tea polyphenol (1.05 ± 0.04 × 10−2) [39], konjac glucomannan/carboxymethyl cellulose (2.92 ± 0.11 × 10−5 g·m−1·s−1·Pa−1) [48], GSE/pea starch (6.67 × 10−10 g·m−1·s−1·Pa−1) [49], and starch/polyvinyl alcohol/curcumin/anthocyanins (1.85 ± 0.22 × 10−10 g·m−1·s−1·Pa−1) [50], and is higher than pullulan and apple fiber (0.56 × 10−10 g·m−1·s−1·Pa−1) [51]. High WVTR can prevent condensation of water vapor within the packaging, and low WVTR can reduce the transfer of moisture from the environment to food [42]. Therefore, the preservation of each kind of fruit requires a suitable resistance to water, which depends on the effect of preservation. For mangoes, an optimal WVTR should balance the need to prevent excessive moisture loss while avoiding condensation inside the packaging. Studies have shown that the appropriate WVTR of mango is about (0.4–2.00) × 10−10 g·m−1·s−1·Pa−1 [26,52]. XHC film achieves a WVTR of (0.59–1.09) × 10−10 g·m−1·s−1·Pa−1, which is within the ideal range for mango preservation. This level of WVTR ensures that the film effectively manages moisture, thereby extending the shelf life of mangoes without compromising their quality.

The biocompatibility results are shown in Fig. 5 . The XHC films exhibited slight detrimental effects on the growth and proliferation of L929 mouse fibroblast cells after 24h of exposure. The cytotoxicity of the XHC film was a slight reduction in cell viability when the concentration increased from 50 to 1000 μg/mL. Even at the maximum concentration of 1000 μg/mL of XHC10 with the largest amount of DE-CQDs addition, the cell viability exceeded 91 %. The results demonstrate that the XHC films have acceptable biocompatibility and are comparatively not toxic to fibroblast cells [53]. These results indicate that the films are suitable for food packaging applications due to their excellent biocompatibility.

Compared to other coatings, the XHC film offers several distinct advantages. Firstly, its excellent UV-blocking properties provide enhanced protection against photooxidation, which is crucial for preserving the nutritional quality of fruits. Secondly, the improved water vapor transmission rate ensures that the film can effectively manage moisture, preventing condensation and mold growth. Lastly, the incorporation of DE-CQDs not only enhances the mechanical properties of the film but also provides antibacterial properties on C. gloeosporioides. These combined benefits make the XHC film a superior choice for mango preservation.

Based on the previous results above, it was found that the XHC film with DE-CQDs can reduce the decay index of mango, and we speculate that this is due to the ability of DE-CQDs to inhibit the growth of pathogens in mango. Therefore, to analyze the preservation mechanism of the XHC film, further verification was carried out by the isolation, characterization, and molecular identification of the mango pathogen and antifungal assays of DE-CQDs. The pure fungal culture of the diseased mango tissue was labeled FJAT-32130. The mycelia were white cottony, the balls yellow, and the microconidia kidney-shaped (Fig. 8a). DNA sequence data generated for FJAT-32130 were submitted to GenBank with accession number OR726223. The FJAT-32130 sequence A was searched by BLAST in GenBank, and the homology of the sequence with C. gloeosporioides strains were 99 % (Fig. 8a). By combining morphological, culture characteristics, and molecular biological data, FJAT-32130 was identified as C. gloeosporioides.

It was found that DE-CQDs have antifungal properties against C. gloeosporioides (Fig. 8b), the colony sizes of C. gloeosporioides treated with DE-CQDs, the negative control, and the positive control are 51.57 ± 0.97a, 59.46 ± 2.26a, and 19.22 ± 3.36c, respectively (P < 0.05). The hyphae treated with DE-CQDs will change from smooth and full to severely shrunk, rough, and flat (Fig. 8c), even showing the phenomenon of apoptosis due to the swollen hyphae (the place indicated by the red arrow) [32]. The findings indicate that the DE-CQDs in this study exhibit inhibitory effects on fungi, underscoring their significant potential for further development in applications as antifungal agents within the realm of food packaging materials [14]. Studies have indicated that the enhanced antimicrobial efficacy of the CQD-incorporated film can be attributed to the potent antimicrobial properties of the CQDs [11].

The apoptosis mechanism of C. gloeosporioides FJAT-32130 treated with DE-CQDs is illustrated in Fig. 9 . As shown in Fig. 9a, strong green fluorescence was detected in the DE-CQDs and Rosup (positive contrast) groups, while no fluorescence was observed in the control group (negative control). This indicates that DE-CQDs promote ROS accumulation in FJAT-32130. This may be due to the -SO₃H group interfering with the redox balance of fungi through the oxidation of thiol groups [14], thereby promoting ROS accumulation, which is a key feature of cell apoptosis [32]. The n → π* transition of the surface C=O bond may also generate reactive oxygen species through a photocatalytic effect. Additionally, the fluorescence properties of DE-CQDs may further enhance ROS generation through a photodynamic effect [56]. A similar phenomenon was observed, where ZnO quantum dots induced oxidative stress in HeLa and HEK-293T cells [57]. The accumulation of intracellular ROS can lead to oxidative stress when their levels exceed the neutralizing capacity of antioxidant systems, damaging DNA, cell membranes, proteins, and other cellular components and ultimately resulting in apoptosis [56]. However, no DNA breaks were detected in the mitochondria of the DE-CQDs-treated group, as evidenced by the absence of green fluorescence in both the control and DE-CQDs-treated groups (Fig. 9b). Based on the two apoptosis pathways in fungi, metacaspase-dependent and metacaspase-independent [32], we investigated the specific mechanisms of apoptosis. As shown in Fig. 9c, DE-CQDs-treated hyphae emitted strong green fluorescence, whereas no fluorescence was observed in the H2O-treated control group, and weakened fluorescence was detected in the caspase inhibitor-treated group. This indicates that DE-CQDs-induced apoptosis in C. gloeosporioides cells was metacaspase-dependent. Functionalized graphene CQDs also induce apoptosis through metacaspase-dependent pathway [58]. In summary, DE-CQDs induce the apoptosis of C. gloeosporioides through the metacaspase-dependent pathway resulting in antifungal activity, thus prolonging the preservation efficacy of the XHC film in mangos.

Our previous research has demonstrated the efficient inhibitory effects of DE-CQDs against plant pathogens. Based on this, we hypothesized that DE-CQDs could also inhibit the growth of the pathogenic fungus C. gloeosporioides on mangoes, thereby extending their shelf life. The results confirmed this hypothesis, showing that DE-CQDs are 2 nm nanoparticles containing functional groups such as -OH, -COOH, -NH₄+, and -SO₃H. Furthermore, the XHC film, which incorporates DE-CQDs, effectively inhibited the growth of C. gloeosporioides and promoted fungal apoptosis. Specifically, DE-CQDs disrupted the mycelial structure of C. gloeosporioides, causing abnormal hyphal morphology, inducing metacaspase-dependent apoptosis, and promoting ROS accumulation without causing DNA fragmentation. Additionally, the XHC film demonstrated excellent compatibility and homogeneity when loaded with DE-CQDs. The incorporation of DE-CQDs enhanced the film's UV-blocking properties, improved the water vapor transmission rate, increased the water contact angle, and ultimately extended the shelf life of mangoes. These findings support our hypothesis that DE-CQDs, when incorporated into XHC films, can serve as an effective antifungal agent to protect mangoes from fungal infections and extend their shelf life. Thus, XHC films can be developed as a promising antifungal coating for mangoes and potentially other fruits susceptible to fungal damage.

However, it is important to acknowledge that the current study is limited by the specific environmental conditions under which the experiments were conducted, and the results may vary under different settings. Furthermore, although the biocompatibility tests yielded promising results, long-term in vivo studies are necessary to comprehensively evaluate the safety and effectiveness of the XHC film for food packaging applications, especially in commercialization phase.

Meixia Zheng: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Meichun Chen: Investigation, Formal analysis. Rongfeng Xiao: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jianwen Feng: Methodology, Investigation. Hailan Su: Methodology, Investigation. Fengfang Lin: Methodology, Investigation. Jianfu Chen: Methodology, Investigation. Kok Bing Tan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yujing Zhu: Conceptualization.
